title: Graphs
description: Build graphs with HashMap adjacency lists, implement DFS and BFS traversals, and find connected components.

sections.title: HashMap Adjacency List Representation
sections.explanation: Graphs are most commonly represented as adjacency lists: `HashMap<Integer, List<Integer>>`. Each key is a node, its value is a list of neighbors. For undirected graphs, add edges in both directions. Build from edge list: for each edge (u,v), add v to graph[u] and u to graph[v]. Use `computeIfAbsent` for clean initialization.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph from edges: {0,1}, {0,2}, {1,2}, {2,3}. Print the neighbors of node 0, the neighbors of node 2, and the total number of nodes in the graph.

validation.message: Use HashMap adjacency list with computeIfAbsent for initialization.

exercise.hint: For each edge, add both directions. computeIfAbsent(node, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(neighbor).

sections.title: Graph DFS Traversal
sections.explanation: DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Use a `HashSet<Integer>` for visited nodes to avoid cycles. Recursive: mark visited, process node, recurse on unvisited neighbors. Iterative: use a stack. DFS is used for path finding, cycle detection, topological sort, and connected components.

exercise.prompt: Build a graph from edges: {0,1}, {0,2}, {1,3}, {2,4}. Implement DFS starting from node 0. Collect visited nodes in order and print the list. Then print the total number of visited nodes.

validation.message: Mark nodes as visited and recurse on unvisited neighbors.

exercise.hint: Add node to visited and order. For each neighbor in graph.getOrDefault(node, []), if not visited, recurse.

sections.title: Graph BFS Traversal
sections.explanation: BFS explores all nodes at distance d before distance d+1. Use a Queue and a visited set. Add start to queue and visited. While queue not empty: poll node, process it, add unvisited neighbors to queue and visited. BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs. Mark visited when adding to queue (not when polling) to avoid duplicates.

exercise.prompt: Build a graph: {0,1}, {0,2}, {1,3}, {1,4}, {2,5}, {2,6}. Implement BFS from node 0. Print the traversal order. This is a tree-like graph so BFS gives level-order: 0, then 1,2, then 3,4,5,6.

validation.message: Use Queue with offer/poll and HashSet for visited tracking.

exercise.hint: Init queue with start, mark visited. While queue not empty: poll, add to order, offer unvisited neighbors.

sections.title: Finding Connected Components
sections.explanation: To find connected components: iterate all nodes. For each unvisited node, run DFS/BFS to find its entire component. Count how many times you start a new DFS/BFS = number of components. Common interview problem: "number of islands", "number of provinces". Time: O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges.

exercise.prompt: Given 7 nodes (0-6) and edges: {0,1}, {0,2}, {3,4}, {5,6}. Find the number of connected components using DFS. Print the component count. Then print the size of the largest component.

validation.message: 3 components: {0,1,2} size 3, {3,4} size 2, {5,6} size 2. Largest is 3.
Use DFS for each unvisited node and Math.max to track the largest component.

exercise.hint: For each node 0-6: if not visited, run dfs (returns component size), increment components, update largest with Math.max.
